segments.text: [MUSIC PLAYING]

CAMERON BALAHAN:
Hi, I'm Cameron,

and I'm the product lead
for the Go programming
language at Google.
MARC DOUGHERTY: And I'm Marc.

I lead developer
relations for Go.

CAMERON BALAHAN: For those of
you who are new to our project,

Go is an open-source programming
language supported by Google

that enables developers
and software engineering

teams to quickly build
more secure, reliable,
and scalable production systems.

Google released Go as
an open-source project

over 15 years ago after starting
it two years earlier in response

to Google's own challenges
with building and maintaining

large-scale,
mission-critical systems.

With existing tools,
we had to choose

between the productivity of
dynamic interpreted languages

and the production readiness
of strongly typed, compiled
languages.

But we wanted both,
so we built Go.

Go's core premise is
that developer tools

should prioritize scale from the
start, which means accounting

for how modern software
is architected,

the environments in which
modern workloads run,

and, most importantly, the
teams that write, operate,
and maintain it all.

So with that in mind,
we framed Go around

three principles that continue
to guide us to this day.
First, Go is productive.

It's simple to learn, simple
to maintain, readable,

and scales well across teams,
workloads, and use cases.

Second, Go is more
than a language.

It's a complete
developer experience.

From the IDE to production, we
provide end-to-end solutions

with touchpoints all across the
software development lifecycle.

And we provide all
of it out of the box

with sensible,
self-tuning defaults.

And third, Go is
production ready.

It's reliable, efficient,
stable, and secure,

which makes it well-suited for
everything from simple apps

to enterprise systems and
critical infrastructure.

Over the years, Go
has found itself

at the center of the modern
cloud and, by extension,
the modern web.

Many of the world's
best-known cloud technologies

are written in Go, including
Kubernetes, Docker, Terraform,
and more.

And companies large and
small, from individuals

to startups to enterprises,
have all embraced Go, especially
for their cloud-based workloads.

And that's in large
part because Go was
purpose built for the cloud.

The libraries and integrations
and architectures that Go enable

were meant for the cloud,
not retrofitted for it later,

which means you can realize
the cloud's benefits faster

and easier than you
can in other languages.

But you don't have to
take my word for it.

Go users consistently give
us extraordinary feedback

and customer
satisfaction-- levels

that are practically
unheard of in the industry.
And the usage shows it, too.

Today, Go is more
popular than ever,

with millions of developers,
and growing fast.

In fact, according to a new
Stack survey from last year,

Go is one of only two
languages growing faster

than the developer
population itself.

The other one's Rust, which we
think pairs really well with Go,

but that's a topic
for another day.

And there are signs like
this everywhere you look.

Go consistently ranks among
the most loved technologies
on Stack Overflow.

And last year,
Cloudflare reported

that Go is the number
one language powering
API calls on the internet.

So whether you're an individual
developer, an enterprise,

or something in
between, Go enables

you to build and scale up
your projects quickly and more
reliably.

And you'll probably
be happy you did.

Next, Marc will dig into
all the latest in Go.
Off to you, Marc.
MARC DOUGHERTY: Thanks, Cameron.

Go releases new major
versions twice a year,
in August and February.

In the last year, we released
a lot of exciting new features

in 1.23 and 1.24 to help you and
your team be more productive.

In 1.23, we introduced iterators
with the seq and seq2 types.

In classic Go fashion,
iterators are more than just

a new type in the
standard library.

They're an elegant
way to simplify loops

using the already familiar
for range expression

and keeping the mechanics
of iterating separate
from the body of your loop.

Before iterators, there were
a couple of different ways
to loop over data.

Some methods would
return a slice containing

all the results, which
can be inefficient
for large collections.

Another way was to create
your own iterator object,

like this code, which uses the
Google Cloud Storage library.
Notice the complexity here.

We have flow control and
error checking in our loop.

And that error checking needs
to be duplicated in every loop.

With iterators, you can
use the familiar for range
syntax to perform the loop.

And the complex flow control
is kept inside the iterator.

This lets our loop
body stay focused

on the handling of our
files or our errors

without worrying
about flow control.

The standard library
as of 1.24 includes

a bunch of iterators
in the strings, slices,
and maps packages.

Because iterators
are just a function,

you can define your own,
including for collection types
defined elsewhere.

Here's the iterator I defined
for the Cloud Storage example.

The declaration
looks a bit complex,

but you can see our
flow control here
has the same effect as before.

This iterator lets us factor
out the flow control handling

from our loop and make
them more readable.

As new concepts like
iterators are introduced,

Go's vertically integrated tools
help keep your code base up

to date with the latest
patterns and idioms.

Go's language server,
gopls, can integrate

with your IDE either with the
language server support found

in most IDEs or
through a plugin,
like the VS Code Go extension.

Gopls helps with the
usual language server

things, like type checking,
function signatures,
and references.
But gopls can do so much more.

Remember that complex
iterator definition?

Since gopls knows about
new features on day one,

it can help you avoid mistakes
while you're writing them.

Here, it's noticed
an error where

our iterator may call
our yield function
after it should have stopped.

gopls includes a suite of
modernizations, common patterns

that have since been
addressed as language features
or standard library additions.

And while you could run the
modernizer over your whole code

base, gopls can suggest them in
line wherever you're editing.

Here, we have some examples
of older patterns on the left

and their modernized
replacements on the right.

That last modernizer showcases
a new feature of the JSON parser
called omitzero.

The JSON package has been
a part of Go since 1.0.

It enables ergonomic development
of API clients and servers

by simplifying
serialization of Go structs.

The addition of
the omitzero option

resolves some common errors
and surprising behavior

when dealing with Go's zero
values, like empty structs

and uninitialized
time.Time objects.

These additions give
you more control

over how your objects
are serialized

to JSON and avoid possible
sources of error and confusion.

Do you need to update
your Go runtime

to take advantage
of a new feature?

As of 1.23, you can use go get
to manage the Go toolchain just
like any other dependency.

Go will download updated
toolchains as needed,

allowing your teams
to use the latest

features without stopping to
update their toolchain manually.

And this applies to
dependencies, too.

If you take a dependency
on code that requires 1.24,

Go will update your
module's go directive

to require 1.24 and
automatically fetch
the 1.24 runtime.

The Go language and
the Go tools continue

to find new ways to
help you keep your code

base readable and modern
and allow your team
to stay focused and productive.

CAMERON BALAHAN:
Marc just walked you

through some of the new features
that make you more productive.

But remember, Go is about
productivity and production
readiness.

So let's talk about the latest
features from Go 1.23 and 1.24

that make your applications more
robust, more secure, and more
performant.

As I said earlier, Go's
founding principles

center in part on
its portability

and a focus on the
modern environments
in which modern workloads run.

These environments
continue to evolve.

And as they do, we want to
be sure that Go keeps up.

One way we've done that
is by significantly

improving Go support for
WebAssembly in Go 1.24.

WebAssembly, or Wasm, is a
binary instruction format

and sandboxed
runtime environment

that unlocks a lot of new
interesting use cases,
especially on the cloud.

Several languages,
including Go, are

capable of compiling
Wasm modules that

contain portable,
architecture-agnostic bytecode
that runs on all Wasm hosts.

The same Wasm host
application can call methods

from multiple different
Wasm modules, which

can be written in one
language or a mix of languages
as appropriate.

These Wasm modules
are hotloadable

and run in a memory-safe,
sandboxed runtime

with structured control
flows and verification.

Any system calls are routed
through the Wasm runtime, which

provides an extra
layer of security,

something like an extremely
lightweight container.

And despite this
layer of abstraction,

Wasm applications are
exceptionally efficient, capable

of achieving near-native
performance on their hosts.

This makes them
particularly useful

for high-performance,
low-latency use cases,
like computing at the edge.

For example, you can
run your Wasm code

on Google Cloud service
extensions, which

provides edge computing at over
200 edge locations across more
than 200 countries.

Go first introduced
support for Wasm in Go 1.11
with the JS Wasm port.

Wasm itself was originally
designed for browsers.

And the JS Wasm port
enabled this use case

by allowing you to target web
pages through a JavaScript host.

Go developers made
some really fun stuff

with this capability,
especially games.

There are even open-source game
engines for Go that leverage
the JS Wasm port.

Go developers can use these
projects to easily develop

impressive 2D games
that run in the browser.
As Wasm evolved, Go did, too.

In Go 1.21, we
introduced support

for preview one of the
WebAssembly System Interface,
or WASI.

WASI provides a
POSIX-style interface

for interacting with
system resources,

like the file system, system
clock, data utilities, and more.

In this example, you can
see a simple "Hello, world!"

program that we compile to Wasm
via the leading compile flags.

We can then run
the program using

one of the many free open-source
Wasm runtimes and libraries.

In this case, we're using
wazero, an open-source project
implemented in Go.

Starting in Go
1.21, Go developers

could build a Wasm
module as an executable,

start it in a Wasm runtime,
and run it to completion.
And that brings us to today.

In Go 1.24, we expanded
Go's Wasm capabilities
in two big ways.

First, Go 1.24 enables you to
export Go functions to the Wasm

host with the go wasmexport
compiler directive.

When we compile code like
this into a Wasm module,

we can import it in a Wasm
host, and the Wasm host

can directly call the
module's exported functions.

Second, Go 1.24 added support
for building WASI reactors.

When you use this capability to
build a Wasm module in Reactor

Mode, the module can
remain initialized

even after it is
finished executing.

This is really useful
for long-running plugins

or extensions that you want
available indefinitely.

Initialize it once,
leave it running,

and it can continue to
respond to calls, including
by maintaining state.

In this example, we use
libraries from wazero

to create a Wasm
host that will call

the add function we exported
in the previous example.

This time, though,
we'll build the Wasm

module in reactor mode with
the highlighted build flag.

Now, we can run the add
function more than once
without reinitializing it.

Next, let's talk
about how Go makes
your applications more secure.

Go has always lead in security
features and capabilities.

In Go 1.13, we introduced
the module proxy and checksum

database, which cache and record
hashes for all dependencies

in the Go ecosystem, protecting
you against man-in-the-middle

attacks and other tampering
of your dependencies.

Then, in Go 1.18, we introduced
built-in fuzz testing,

the first major programming
language with native fuzz

testing built into
and integrated
with its standard toolchain.

You can think of fuzzing as
a form of automated testing

that intelligently manipulates
inputs to your program

to flush out bugs, especially
security vulnerabilities.

And in 2022, we introduced
Go's end-to-end vulnerability

management system, which
surfaces known vulnerabilities

in your dependencies everywhere,
from your IDE to the runtime.

By analyzing the call
graph from your code

through your dependencies, Go's
vulnerability management tools

are able to detect
whether you actually

invoke vulnerable code,
eliminating the vast majority
of false positives.

Building on our security
focus, in Go 1.24,

we introduced support for
post-quantum cryptography,

all of it implemented
transparently behind the scenes.

We also improved
support for FIPS-140,

as US government
compliance regime

that includes
approved algorithms
for cryptographic applications.

You can enable FIPS mode without
any changes to your code,

either at runtime with
the highlighted debug flag

or at build time with the
highlighted build flag.

Finally, we've continued
to focus on making
Go faster and more efficient.

One important way
we've done this

is through the introduction of
a completely new implementation

of the built-in map type based
on a new hash table design
called Swiss Tables.

Beginning in Go 1.24,
maps transparently

use the new Swiss
Table implementation.

In microbenchmarks,
map operations

using the new
implementation are up to 60%

faster than they were in Go
1.23, especially for large maps.

And it's all seamlessly
integrated in Go's built-in map.
No need to adjust your code.
Just upgrade, and you're set.

And there's more, including
lots of new low-level tools

across both Go 1.23 and
1.24 for efficiency.

For example, in Go 1.23, we
introduced the Unique Package,

enabling the efficient
deduplication and comparison
of values.

In Go 1.24, we
introduced weak.Pointers,

which enable you to
safely point to an object

without preventing it from
being garbage collected, as well

as the AddCleanup function,
a finalization mechanism that

is more flexible, more
efficient, and less error prone.

And there's more, including
improved memory allocation

speeds and general speed
improvements throughout.

And all of it
continues our focus

on keeping Go both productive
and production ready.

Next, let's turn it back
to Marc for a quick rundown

on the latest in Go's
use in generative AI.

MARC DOUGHERTY: As
you've just heard,

Go is packed with
features that make

it a great language
for production systems.

Features like efficient
networking libraries

and integrated
struct tags make it

great for building
distributed systems.

And that's a big part of why
Go is so prevalent in cloud
infrastructure and services.

And those same
reasons are why Go

is a great choice for building
AI infrastructure and services
today.

Popular generative AI
tools and libraries,

like Ollama, Local AI, LangChain
Go, Genkit, and many others,
are all written in Go.

Like the major infrastructure
projects before them,

these tools and libraries
leverage Go's productivity

and production readiness to
create highly scalable and more

reliable
mission-critical services

that millions of developers
from various language ecosystems

rely upon for their
AI-powered workloads.

In fact, cloud and AI
systems have more in common
than you might think.

Since LLMs often
require dedicated,

specialized compute
resources, they're

usually run as a network
service called through an API.

Let's consider the
example of a retrieval

augmented generation system
as outlined in a recent post
on the Go blog.

Our RAG system uses
a vector database

to store relevant documents
to feed to our LLM

when answering the
user's questions.

Vector databases rely on a
specialized embedding model

so we can efficiently query
for documents that are
similar to the user's question.

We'll look at three
different frameworks

we can use to connect
these services together.

For our first example, we'll use
the Gemini and Weaviate client
libraries directly.

This code comes from
the user query handler.

We're using Weaviate's GraphQL
interface to fetch documents.

And the query itself
is a bit long,
so we used a helper function.

One downside to this
approach is that we'd

have to rewrite our helper if
we change our vector database.

Here, we're using
LangChain Go, which

provides interface abstractions
for our LLM and vector database.

The similarity
search and generate

from single prompt
calls won't need

to change if we replace
those components.

Last, we'll look at
Firebase Genkit for Go,
currently in beta.

It provides similar
abstractions to LangChain Go.

Genkit includes production-grade
features like prompt management

and observability that may
not be visible in the code

but improve the overall
developer experience.

As your AI systems grow,
Go's emphasis on simplicity

means your code remains
readable even as it
grows in size and complexity.

Go features like object
embedding and interfaces

allow for seamless migrations
as requirements and technology
change, and they always change.

Go's proven ability to
keep up with rapid change

has made it successful in some
of the best-known components
of cloud Infrastructure.

The same features that
drove Go adoption in cloud

make it an excellent choice as
we build the AI infrastructure
of tomorrow.

CAMERON BALAHAN: As I hope we've
demonstrated in this video,

Go's founding principles
around productivity, developer

experience, and
production readiness

are still how we
prioritize our work today.

Before we conclude, I want to
take a few minutes to give you

a peek at what's around the
corner in Go 1.25 and beyond.

First, and building on
Marc's discussion around AI,

we're very excited about the
work we're doing around SIMD.

SIMD enables modern CPUs
to perform vectorized array

manipulations, running certain
kinds of loops in parallel.

These capabilities are essential
for many kinds of performance

optimizations, including the
sort required for certain kinds
of AI infrastructure.

Rounding out our
performance work,

we have a lot of
exciting opportunities

around multicore hardware,
including garbage collector

and scheduler
capabilities that make

better use of nonuniform
memory access in modern CPU
architectures.

Switching to the
language itself,

we have a lot of
polish ahead of us

in our continuing push to
improve productivity, especially

around flexibility in
generic operations.

For more information
on that work,

check out our discussions
on the Go project on GitHub.

And as we do all
this and more, you

can rest assured that
any changes we make now

and in the future will
continue to fulfill the Go
promise of compatibility.

Go remains and will always
remain fully backward compatible
to Go 1.0.

As we wrap up, we
want to take a moment
to thank the Go community.

We, the Go team, are
committed to keeping

Go productive and production
ready well into the future.
But we know we aren't alone.

Today, our ecosystem is larger
and more robust than ever.

And we continue to see a lot
of really high-quality tools

and libraries emerge,
especially for new use
cases around generative AI.

And we see hundreds of
thousands of gophers

around the world meeting up,
attending Go conferences,

and collaborating online
all because they love Go.

So thank you to
the Go community.

It's because of
your contributions

that Go is growing and more
relevant than ever before.

We're very proud to be a part
of this journey with you.

To get started, or for more
information on anything

we discussed in this
video, be sure to check out
our homepage at go.dev.

Thank you for joining us
this year at Google I/O.

We can't wait to see what
you build with Go this year
and in the years to come.
[MUSIC PLAYING]
